jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana debating what is 'witchy' as Abbi is being escorted into what is clearly an abortion clinic while protesters shout in the background
jokes.setup: The scene appears to be a casual hangout conversation about hairstyles and aesthetics
jokes.punchline: The obliviousness of the witchy conversation is thrown into surreal relief by the abortion clinic context and screaming protesters — the show never acknowledges the protesters as unusual, treating reproductive healthcare as mundanely normal
jokes.explanation: Earns its high score through conceptual boldness and perfect character integration. The show treats an abortion clinic visit with complete normalcy — Ilana's coaching ('you are a strong queen, your body your choice') is delivered with the same energy she'd use for any errand, and then the conversation immediately pivots back to witch aesthetics. The juxtaposition of casual girl-talk over screaming protesters is the joke, and it works because it reflects exactly who these characters are: they genuinely don't experience this as a dramatic event. The normalizing framing is itself a political and comedic statement. High craft, high impact.

jokes.text: 'Witch shit is fucking hot, dawg.' / 'Hot dog!'
jokes.setup: Ilana says 'witch shit is fucking hot, dawg'
jokes.punchline: Abbi riffs on 'hot' + 'dawg' = 'hot dog' — a completely stupid, delightful non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: A perfectly executed micro-joke. The wordplay is dumb in exactly the right way, and the speed of Abbi's response suggests she couldn't help herself — which is very true to how these two communicate. The economy is near-perfect: three syllables, big laugh. Doesn't reach exceptional but is a clean, well-landed bit of verbal improv energy.

jokes.text: The 'what is witchy?' list: clogs, long nails, short nails, soup, flutes — delivered completely earnestly while protesters shout in the background
jokes.setup: Continuing the witchy aesthetics conversation mid-protest
jokes.punchline: 'Short nails, soup.' and 'Flutes?' / 'Yas, flutes.' — the list escalates from plausibly witchy (clogs) to completely unhinged (soup, flutes) with full conviction
jokes.explanation: The joke is the absolute sincerity with which both characters engage with this non-logic. 'Short nails, soup' is genuinely funny because short nails are the opposite of a witch aesthetic and soup is just a food. The inclusion of 'flutes' and Ilana's immediate 'yas' to confirm it is a strong beat. This is peak Broad City improv energy — the comedy lives in the commitment, not the words themselves.

jokes.text: Protester shouts 'What if they had aborted baby Jesus?' as a parting shot
jokes.setup: Anti-abortion protesters are shouting at the clinic
jokes.punchline: The specific theological absurdity of invoking Jesus as an anti-abortion argument gets one crisp line — the show doesn't belabor it, just lets it land and cuts
jokes.explanation: A familiar joke about religious protesters, elevated slightly by the show's commitment to breezing past it rather than editorializing. Not a novel observation but efficiently deployed. Low character integration since it's a one-line throwaway from a nameless protester.

jokes.text: Abbi is visibly caught in a compromising or lazy position at her new job and covers by saying she was 'checking out the chair, making sure the height was right'
jokes.setup: Dara catches Abbi doing something she shouldn't be at her new assistant job
jokes.punchline: The feeble lie about 'checking the chair height' — an implausible excuse delivered with Abbi's signature over-explaining panic energy
jokes.explanation: A functional character-comedy beat. Abbi's bad-liar energy is one of her core comedic traits and this is a clean example. Nothing remarkable but it's well-executed shorthand for her character's anxious people-pleasing.

jokes.text: Abbi excitedly presents brochure mock-ups she spent the night on; Dara ignores them entirely and asks about the kitty litter
jokes.setup: Abbi has gone above and beyond creatively for her job, clearly hoping for praise
jokes.punchline: Dara cuts her off mid-sentence to ask about organic kitty litter — Abbi's ambitions are irrelevant; she is a cat-litter-buying assistant
jokes.explanation: Strong structural comedy — the setup is Abbi's eager overachieving and the punchline is the complete dismissal of it. The specificity of 'organic kitty litter' as the thing that matters more than her creative work is a nice detail. This is a clean articulation of the assistant-as-invisible-person dynamic that pays off Abbi's arc in this episode.

jokes.text: 'Rock star.' — Dara's praise for Abbi managing to locate the cat food
jokes.setup: Abbi has just accomplished a trivial task (finding cat food and partially managing shoe repairs)
jokes.punchline: Dara calls her a 'rock star' with apparent sincerity — the gap between the praise and the achievement is the joke
jokes.explanation: A clean, quick irony beat. Works within the scene's running joke about Abbi's thwarted ambitions, but is too familiar a comic device to score high on originality.

jokes.text: 'I love my new job.' — Abbi's declaration immediately after being tasked with cat food and cobbler errands
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been given a series of menial, trivial tasks while her creative work was ignored
jokes.punchline: She declares she loves her job — the irony is unclear (is she deluding herself? is she genuinely relieved to not be at Soulstice?) — the ambiguity makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: The ambiguity of sincerity vs. self-delusion is what elevates this above a simple irony beat. Abbi's character is defined by wanting to be recognized, so her genuine contentment here is both funny and a little sad.

jokes.text: Package delivery guy informs Abbi she doesn't work at 'Soul-stice' anymore — mispronouncing it as two separate words
jokes.setup: Phone call about a misdirected package
jokes.punchline: 'Soul-stice' — the delivery guy's mispronunciation turns the pretentious gym name into something even funnier by splitting it wrong
jokes.explanation: A small but effective beat. The mispronunciation pokes fun at the gym's pretentious branding while also characterizing the delivery guy. Works as texture rather than a standout joke.

jokes.text: Delivery guy offers to either destroy the package or send it to 'North Brother Island' — making a mundane redirect sound like a threat
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if the package can be redirected
jokes.punchline: 'Nah, I could have it destroyed or taken to our distribution center on North Brother Island' — the total indifference and the gothic name 'North Brother Island' lands as a weirdly sinister non-option
jokes.explanation: North Brother Island is a real NYC location (a desolate, abandoned island in the East River), which makes this joke land harder for New Yorkers while working as pure absurdism for everyone else. The bureaucratic nihilism of 'destroyed or island' is a strong comedic beat. The delivery guy's complete indifference is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: 'What up, my fellow Ashkenazi Jew?'
jokes.setup: Ilana picks up the phone for what sounds like a normal emergency call from Abbi
jokes.punchline: Ilana's greeting is 'What up, my fellow Ashkenazi Jew?' — specific, ethnically self-aware, and completely unearned as an opener
jokes.explanation: Near-perfectly character-integrated. This greeting could only come from Ilana — it's simultaneously affectionate, identity-proud, and absurd as a phone salutation. The specificity of 'Ashkenazi' rather than just 'Jew' is the key detail that makes it land. High quotability because of the specificity. Strong craft score because it does a lot of character work in four words.

jokes.text: Ilana lists the reasons Abbi can't go back to Soulstice: 'because you crushed Trey and fully disrespected his existence? Or because you staged your own Publisher's Clearinghouse at the gym?'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she can't go back to Soulstice
jokes.punchline: Ilana rattles off the two specific reasons with total recall and no judgment — 'Publisher's Clearinghouse' is a perfect absurd callback to what was apparently a previous episode event
jokes.explanation: The joke lives in Ilana's encyclopedic, non-judgmental cataloguing of Abbi's disasters. 'Staged your own Publisher's Clearinghouse at the gym' is a brilliant specific image for what was apparently an absurd exit. The rhythm of the two options and Abbi's correction ('it was Powerball, but both') is textbook two-hander comedy.

jokes.text: 'Uh, it was Powerball, but both.'
jokes.setup: Ilana calls out Abbi's Publisher's Clearinghouse stunt
jokes.punchline: Abbi corrects only the brand name — not the fact that she faked a lottery win — treating the lie itself as unremarkable while being precise about the specifics
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect micro-joke. The comedic logic is: Abbi's priorities are completely backwards — she doesn't address the insane behavior, only the incorrect brand name. 'But both' as the addendum confirms she's fully aware of her own disaster. Maximum laughs per word. This is the kind of tight, character-specific writing that defines the show at its best. Justifiably scores above 8 on craft.

jokes.text: Ilana is eating a baked potato cut in half, described as 'a lunch potato and a dinner potato'
jokes.setup: Abbi notices something odd about what Ilana is eating
jokes.punchline: 'It's cut in half, so it's a lunch potato and a dinner potato' — Ilana applies maximum fiscal logic to minimum food; the poverty joke is wrapped in complete self-satisfaction
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The image of a single baked potato cut in half and labeled as two separate meals is both funny and quietly devastating — it's the broke millennial experience distilled to one prop. What makes it exceptional is Ilana's complete pride in the logic. She has not failed; she has engineered a solution. This is high-quality character comedy that also does economic commentary. Highly quotable, strong visual gag component, and could only come from Ilana. Earns its 8.5 score.

jokes.text: 'So freakin' broke, dude. / So broke!'
jokes.setup: Ilana explains she has an interview she can't skip because she needs money.
jokes.punchline: Both characters echo the sentiment simultaneously, as if broke-ness is a shared identity they're proud of.
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat — their shared financial precarity as bonding. Not particularly original but authentic to the show's world.

jokes.text: 'Well, good luck, m' witch.'
jokes.setup: Ilana says goodbye to Abbi
jokes.punchline: 'M' witch' as a term of endearment — a callback to the opening witchy conversation, now repurposed as their version of 'my bitch'
jokes.explanation: A neat little callback that earns its place. The transformation of 'my witch' into a term of endearment that phonetically echoes 'm'lady' or 'my bitch' is a small but genuinely clever linguistic move. Very Ilana. High economy, strong character integration. The rewatch bonus applies because it rewards viewers who caught the setup.

jokes.text: Abbi lies to Dara that her parents are getting divorced to get out of work — the lie is obviously made up on the spot
jokes.setup: Abbi needs to leave work to retrieve her package from Soulstice
jokes.punchline: She invents a parental divorce in real time, with clearly zero conviction — the comedy is in watching Abbi's self-defeating pattern of lying continue into her new job
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy that serves the episode's thematic spine (Abbi is a liar). Gets a rewatch bonus because the lie is the exact character flaw Abbi will be called out on later. Not exceptional on its own but lands cleanly.

jokes.text: 'Thank you for being so honest about your need for mental help.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just lied to her boss about needing emergency therapy
jokes.punchline: Dara thanks her for her 'honesty' — the word choice is perfect because Abbi was lying, and yet Dara's framing also lands as a slightly tone-deaf compliment about therapy
jokes.explanation: A crisp double-irony beat. 'Honest' ironizes Abbi's lie while simultaneously reading as a parody of wellness-culture workplace speak ('we celebrate mental health openness here'). The rewatch bonus: on second viewing you realize the show is setting up its honesty theme through Dara praising Abbi's 'honesty' right after she lies.

jokes.text: Restaurant manager's first words to Ilana: 'We're not open. Get out.'
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives for her job interview
jokes.punchline: Marcel's greeting is maximally hostile before she's said a word — establishes his character immediately
jokes.explanation: Economy of character introduction. 'We're not open. Get out.' in four words establishes Marcel as a tyrant, sets up the power dynamic, and gets a laugh. Not especially original but very efficiently done.

jokes.text: Marcel asking Ilana 'Oh, you're friends with the little Guatemalan boy' to refer to Jaime
jokes.setup: Ilana mentions a referral for the interview
jokes.punchline: Marcel reduces Jaime to 'the little Guatemalan boy' — the descriptor is equal parts dismissive, specific, and accidentally affectionate in a way that's funny precisely because it's so wrong
jokes.explanation: The joke walks a line — the humor is in Marcel's imperious obliviousness, not in the descriptor itself. The show gets away with it because Marcel is clearly the absurd villain of his own domain. Works as character establishment for Marcel.

jokes.text: 'Yes, but Dumper's Post was way more sophisticated than it sounds.'
jokes.setup: Marcel is reviewing Ilana's resume; she lists experience at 'Dumper's Post' on Long Island
jokes.punchline: Ilana's preemptive defense — 'more sophisticated than it sounds' — confirms that it is in fact exactly what it sounds like
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Dumper's Post' is inspired — it's a name so perfectly awful that the denial makes it worse. 'More sophisticated than it sounds' applied to something called Dumper's Post is a classic comedic self-defeat. Character-specific because only Ilana would have a resume that includes a place called Dumper's Post and also feel the need to defend it.

jokes.text: 'I also served at the Golden Heirloom, a home for the elderly. I would birdfeed them tapioca.'
jokes.setup: Ilana continues listing her experience
jokes.punchline: 'I would birdfeed them tapioca' — the verb 'birdfeed' applied to elderly people eating tapioca is specific, dark, and perfectly visual
jokes.explanation: Standout joke. 'Birdfeed' as a verb is the discovery here — it's not a word one uses for humans, and applying it to elderly people eating tapioca creates a specific, slightly dehumanizing but not cruel image that is very funny. The word choice implies spooning food from above into open mouths, which is both visual and dark without being mean-spirited. It's the kind of specific, weird verb that makes a joke impossible to forget. Earns its 8+ score.

jokes.text: Marcel declares: 'The only way to make it in the world of Manhattan restauranteries is to be a Kween of Mean'
jokes.setup: Marcel is explaining his philosophy of service
jokes.punchline: 'Restauranteries' — a made-up word that somehow communicates exactly the pretentious Manhattan dining scene Marcel inhabits, paired with 'Kween of Mean' (a RuPaul-inflected coinage)
jokes.explanation: 'Restauranteries' is a perfect made-up word — it feels like exactly the kind of portmanteau a self-important Manhattan GM would coin. 'Kween of Mean' as a drag-culture inflected management philosophy is funny because of the gap between the fabulous framing and the actual sadism it describes. Marcel is a great comic creation.

jokes.text: 'Look, why don't you go work in a Connecticut Muffin, honey. And when you turn into a Conneti fucking muffin, you can come back to Sushi Mambeaux.'
jokes.setup: Marcel is dismissing Ilana as unqualified
jokes.punchline: The insult uses Connecticut Muffin (a real NYC cafe chain) as shorthand for basic, then escalates to 'turn into a Connecticut fucking muffin' — the threat/transformation metaphor is completely unhinged
jokes.explanation: Marcel's insults work because they escalate past conventional disses into surreal territory. The idea of 'becoming' a Connecticut muffin is genuinely strange comedy. 'Sushi Mambeaux' as the restaurant name is also funny — it's trying too hard at fusion cool, which perfectly characterizes the place. The NY-insider reference to Connecticut Muffin adds a layer for local viewers.

jokes.text: Ilana fights back: 'You're a general manager at a restaurant with a B health grade, so maybe you wanna drop the fucking attitude.'
jokes.setup: Marcel has been condescending and tried to dismiss Ilana
jokes.punchline: Ilana cites the B health grade as the specific receipts that deflate his pomposity — NYC diners know a B grade is a real mark against a restaurant
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect comeback. The genius is the specificity — not just 'it's a bad restaurant' but 'a B health grade,' which is a very precise NYC detail that implies actual rodent/roach incidents. The comeback is satisfying because it's earned: Ilana waited until she had specific ammunition, then used it clinically. The structure is flawless (condescension → specific deflation → profanity for emphasis). Earns 8+ because it's the kind of comeback that makes an audience cheer.

jokes.text: 'And just because Sex & the City filmed here 25 years ago one time, doesn't mean that I need this'
jokes.setup: Ilana continues her takedown of Marcel and the restaurant
jokes.punchline: The 'Sex & the City filmed here 25 years ago one time' detail — the precision of '25 years ago one time' demolishes any lingering prestige; it's so specifically pathetic
jokes.explanation: A great NYC comedy beat. The number of Manhattan establishments that trade on exactly this type of aging pop culture credential is real, and Ilana's specificity ('one time') is the knife. Works beautifully as the second punch in a two-punch combo with the health grade line.

jokes.text: Marcel: 'You're hired.' — immediately after Ilana tells him 'fuck you and your Manhattan restauranteries'
jokes.setup: The interview appears to have gone catastrophically wrong; Ilana has told him off
jokes.punchline: 'You're hired.' — the complete reversal reveals this was Marcel's test all along; being mean IS the qualification
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. The scene has been building toward Ilana's failure, making the reversal genuinely surprising. It also perfectly reinforces Marcel's earlier speech — 'be a Kween of Mean' — making the hiring feel both absurd and logical simultaneously. Two words, maximum comedy. The rewatch bonus is strong because on second viewing, you see Marcel testing her from the start. Earns 8+ on craft.

jokes.text: Abbi arrives at Soulstice to pick up her package and the front desk person is immediately dismissive: 'This isn't a post office. This is a gym for people to work out.'
jokes.setup: Abbi approaches the Soulstice front desk, nervous about being back
jokes.punchline: The front desk's condescending explanation of what a gym is — as if Abbi doesn't know — lands as a perfectly petty customer service put-down
jokes.explanation: A functional cringe beat. The humor in 'I know what a gym is' coming from Abbi (a trainer) is solid but not exceptional. More of a scene-setter than a standout joke.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Yeah, I know what a gym is.'
jokes.setup: Front desk explains it's 'a gym for people to work out'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat, controlled response — she's a personal trainer and this is her former workplace; the patience required to say this line without screaming is the joke
jokes.explanation: The bite in this line comes entirely from context — Abbi is a certified trainer being condescended to about what a gym is. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Trey advising a client about steroids: 'I wouldn't recommend steroids for you yet... But we'll see where we wanna go. You wanna get really big, where you can lift a dancer, we might think about it.'
jokes.setup: Trey appears to be giving legitimate training advice
jokes.punchline: The casual 'we might think about steroids' as a future goal, and the specific metric of 'lift a dancer' as the strength benchmark — completely insane personal trainer advice delivered with full professional confidence
jokes.explanation: A great Trey joke. His character is a well-meaning but fundamentally unqualified trainer, and 'we'll consider steroids when you can lift a dancer' is peak Trey — aspirational about the wrong things, completely sincere, and totally inappropriate. 'Lift a dancer' as a fitness goal is inspired specificity. Earns 8+ because it's a great character joke that uses Trey perfectly.

jokes.text: Ilana hangs up on Abbi mid-call
jokes.setup: Abbi calls excitedly about Shania
jokes.punchline: Ilana just hangs up — she has heard this Shania lie too many times to engage; the call dropping is immediate and total
jokes.explanation: The economy here is the entire joke — no words needed. Ilana's judgment is expressed purely through action. Works because of how perfectly it characterizes their relationship: Ilana's love for Abbi is absolute but she will not indulge this particular delusion.

jokes.text: 'No, I... I hung up on you.' / 'Dude, you cannot keep lying about this Shania shit. It's getting weird.'
jokes.setup: Abbi calls back after being hung up on, assuming bad service
jokes.punchline: Ilana clarifies she deliberately hung up, then delivers the 'it's getting weird' judgment — the escalation from 'lie' to 'weird' is specific and funny
jokes.explanation: The 'I hung up on you' clarification is a clean comedy beat — direct and a little brutal. The callout about the lie 'getting weird' is specific to their relationship dynamic.

jokes.text: 'Sorry, I'm training for my new job as a bitch waitress, and I'm getting really good at it.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is being unusually harsh with Abbi on the phone
jokes.punchline: She reveals she's been practicing being mean for her new job — treating Abbi as an unwilling rehearsal partner — with full self-awareness and apparent pride in her progress
jokes.explanation: 'Bitch waitress' as a job title is a great compound noun. The premise — practicing meanness on your best friend and reporting your progress — is very Ilana. The rewatch value is strong because now you retroactively read her phone manner as performance. Earns 8+ on craft because of how character-specific it is and the cleverness of the callback to Marcel's hiring speech.

jokes.text: 'Your most egregious lie was Shania, 'cause that was to me. You're my pretty little liar and that's the truth.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is cataloguing Abbi's recent lies
jokes.punchline: 'You're my pretty little liar and that's the truth' — the construction 'and that's the truth' appended to 'pretty little liar' is an intentional irony that also functions as an expression of love
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever line. The self-referential irony of 'that's the truth' following 'liar' is well-constructed, and the affectionate framing ('my pretty little liar') shows the Abbi-Ilana relationship at its warmest. Also sets up the episode's thematic spine.

jokes.text: Abbi gets caught trying to secretly photograph Shania and is immediately put in a headlock by Maria, Shania's bodyguard
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to get proof that Shania is real and attempts to sneak a photo
jokes.punchline: She is immediately and efficiently neutralized by a bodyguard — the gap between Abbi's sneaky intention and the instant, violent response is the joke
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works primarily through Abbi's complete failure at stealth. Gets visual energy from the bodyguard's efficient response. Solid if unspectacular.

jokes.text: Shania requests 'How about 15?' when Trey says 'take five' — then sings 'Man, I feel like a smoothie'
jokes.setup: Trey tells Shania to 'take five'
jokes.punchline: Shania bargains for 15 minutes, then exits to the smoothie bar singing a parody of her own most famous song
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. 'Man, I feel like a smoothie' is a perfect celebrity cameo joke — it requires that the celebrity be completely game to parody themselves, and the parody is precisely one word different from the original in a way that makes perfect situational sense. It's also charming rather than embarrassing, which is the hardest thing to pull off in a celebrity self-reference. The '15 minutes' extension first is a great tiny escalation that sets up the song exit. Earns 8+ clearly.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I didn't win Powerball.' / Trey: 'Yeah, I know, that's why I did this.' / Abbi: 'Does everybody know?' / Trey: 'I think so, air quotes are pretty big now.'
jokes.setup: Trey uses air quotes when saying Abbi 'won Powerball'
jokes.punchline: Abbi asks if everyone knows; Trey deadpans 'air quotes are pretty big now' — his gentle condescension about air quotes as a communication technology is deeply funny
jokes.explanation: 'Air quotes are pretty big now' is an excellent line. The joke is that Trey is explaining a ubiquitous non-verbal signal as if Abbi has never encountered it, delivered with perfect calm. It's condescending in the most gentle, oblivious way possible, which is exactly Trey's register. The structure — earnest question, dry answer — is clean. Earns 8+ for the perfection of 'pretty big now' specifically.

jokes.text: 'I knew Top Gun would work.'
jokes.setup: Trey says 'okay' to Abbi's request after she invoked Top Gun.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's private victory — confirming that Top Gun always works on Trey, which both characters knew.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the Top Gun exchange — Abbi knew it would work, Trey knew she knew, and he said yes anyway. The self-satisfied delivery implied by the line gives Abbi a small comedic victory.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I knew Top Gun would work.' / Trey: 'Don't expect anyone to learn your name.'
jokes.setup: Abbi convinced Trey by saying 'I could be your Goose' — a Top Gun reference
jokes.punchline: Trey warns her not to expect to be recognized — which is also a Top Gun reference (Goose dies) but played completely straight as a workplace warning
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: as dry workplace advice and as a Top Gun callback (Goose dies anonymously). Trey's total sincerity in delivering it is what makes it land — he may or may not intend the double meaning, and the ambiguity is funny.

jokes.text: Marcel introducing staff: 'That's Owen. I call him Valtrex. He has herpes. No shade, most people have herpes.'
jokes.setup: Marcel is introducing Ilana to the restaurant staff with their nicknames
jokes.punchline: 'No shade, most people have herpes' — the responsible STI awareness addendum after giving someone a humiliating herpes-based nickname is the joke; Marcel is simultaneously cruel and weirdly considerate
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is perfect: the cruelty (nickname 'Valtrex') → the explanation (he has herpes) → the rider ('no shade, most people have herpes'). The last line is what makes it exceptional — Marcel pivoting to public health messaging immediately after humiliating someone is completely insane and also somewhat factually accurate (HSV-1 prevalence is high), which adds another layer. This kind of joke is only possible from Marcel's specific character — imperious, fabulous, and operating by his own ethical code. High craft, highest impact in the episode. Earns its 8.7.

jokes.text: 'That's Brenda. She doesn't have a nickname, wouldn't allow it. Seniority. She came with the building.'
jokes.setup: Marcel continues staff introductions
jokes.punchline: 'She came with the building' — Brenda's seniority is so absolute she predates the restaurant concept; she is an immovable structural feature
jokes.explanation: 'She came with the building' is a brilliant piece of writing. It takes the idea of extreme seniority and translates it into real estate language — Brenda is not an employee, she is a fixture. Maximum economy (five words), high originality in the framing, and it sets up Brenda as a character perfectly. Earns 8+ for the economy and the genius of the real estate metaphor.

jokes.text: 'If you're the right fit, your nickname will find you.'
jokes.setup: Marcel has been assigning/explaining staff nicknames
jokes.punchline: Marcel frames the nickname as something organic and fated — 'it will find you' — giving his arbitrary cruelty the gravity of destiny
jokes.explanation: A great closing line for the staff introduction sequence. Marcel treating his hazing nicknames as a mystical process — 'the nickname will find you' — is perfectly in character. Gets a rewatch bonus because Ilana's nickname does indeed 'find her' later.

jokes.text: Abbi introduces herself to Shania Twain and Shania already knows her name — 'Abbi.' — a beat before Abbi says it
jokes.setup: Trey says 'Hey, Shania, this is...' and pauses
jokes.punchline: Shania says 'Abbi' before Trey can — she apparently already knows Abbi by reputation, which re-contextualizes the legend of Abbi's Shania training lie and Shania's awareness of the situation
jokes.explanation: A perfect one-word punch. Shania knowing Abbi's name collapses multiple layers — Abbi has been lying about knowing Shania for so long that somehow Shania has heard about Abbi. The structural economy (one word, maximum implication) is excellent. Earns 8+ because it pays off a multi-season running gag with a single syllable.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'You have no idea how many times I have said that this has happened.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just met the real Shania Twain
jokes.punchline: Abbi's first words aren't 'wow' or 'hi' — they're a confession about her lie; she immediately makes Shania Twain aware that she has been impersonating her, delivered with complete overwhelmed honesty
jokes.explanation: A great Abbi moment. This is not what anyone says when they meet a celebrity — instead of fangirling, Abbi immediately confesses her lie to Shania's face. It's the most Abbi possible response, it's character-specific, and the structure is great (Shania has no context for this statement). Earns 8+ because it's a perfect character joke in the best tradition of Broad City.

jokes.text: Shania, Abbi, and Trey bonding over Friends: 'I'm such a Chandler.' / 'Total Rachel.' / 'I'm such a Ross.' — with Shania also being 'A little bit Phoebe' and 'A little Gunther, also'
jokes.setup: The three have been distracted from working out and start talking about wanting to watch Friends
jokes.punchline: Each person's Friends character self-identification reveals character: Shania as Phoebe (makes sense) and Gunther (unexpected) is the funniest — nobody identifies as Gunther
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates nicely: everyone's Friends identification is legible, then Shania adds 'a little Gunther' which is the true payoff — Gunther is the peripheral sad crush character, a deeply unexpected choice for Shania Twain. The whole scene works because of the warmth of three very different people finding common ground over a TV show, and the comedy comes from the shared procrastination.

jokes.text: Trey does a Ross impression to get out of doing the stair climber, then has to do it anyway
jokes.setup: Shania says she'll do the stair climber if Trey does his Ross impression
jokes.punchline: Trey does the impression (we get the impression from his enthusiastic 'Uh, uh, uh, uh... Fine by me!') and still has to do the climber
jokes.explanation: A decent physical comedy beat. Trey getting conned into both the impression and the climber is a small satisfying reversal. The humor is mostly in performance.

jokes.text: Marcel and Ilana's interaction about 'petit uni': 'If you have to ask, it's probably not for you-ni.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks what 'petit uni' is on the menu
jokes.punchline: 'Probably not for you-ni' — Marcel transforms 'you' into 'you-ni' to complete the pun, a snobbish gatekeeping remark with a built-in linguistic twist
jokes.explanation: 'Not for you-ni' is a tight wordplay-snobbery combo. The pun requires Marcel to be doing a restaurant snob bit AND making a linguistic joke simultaneously, which is in perfect character. High economy, high quotability.

jokes.text: Two customers ordering: 'My dad owns H&M. We'll take one of everything, you whore.' / 'Played you, bitch.'
jokes.setup: Two customers are trying to prove they belong at the upscale restaurant by ordering extravagantly and using Ilana's insults back on her
jokes.punchline: 'My dad owns H&M' as a flex — H&M is fast fashion, not luxury — and 'Played you, bitch' after ordering one of everything is aggressively overstated triumph
jokes.explanation: 'My dad owns H&M' is the key detail — H&M is democratic fast fashion, not a luxury brand, so it's a terrible flex that the customer delivers with total confidence. The scene works as a parody of the restaurant's pretension game: the 'mean waitress' bit has created a specific customer subculture.

jokes.text: Ilana's inner conflict about being mean: 'This isn't you! You lift people up, you don't tear them down!'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been successfully practicing being mean at her new job
jokes.punchline: She breaks and gives herself an inspirational-poster speech — the gap between the required meanness and Ilana's genuine warmth comes to a head in the most Ilana possible way (talking to herself out loud)
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy. Ilana's absolute inability to sustain cruelty even for financial survival is perfectly in character. The inspirational-speech framing is a nice touch.

jokes.text: 'Marcel, um... I'm really struggling to be mean. This just isn't me. Wow. I... I have to quit.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's crisis reaches Marcel
jokes.punchline: Ilana's earnest 'I have to quit' on the basis of being too nice is a funny confession — the show that started with her trying to be a bitch waitress ends with her unable to sustain it for one training session
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — the setup promised this would be hard for Ilana, and it delivers. The rapid quit is funny but a bit predictable by this point.

jokes.text: Ilana accidentally knocks Brenda over, and Brenda immediately screams 'You did it on purpose, you bitch!'
jokes.setup: Ilana bends to pick up Brenda's dropped scarf; Brenda falls
jokes.punchline: Brenda's immediate, absolute conviction that this was intentional — 'you did it on purpose, you bitch!' — before any investigation or deliberation
jokes.explanation: Brenda's paranoia is the joke — she has apparently been in the restaurant so long she assumes all violence is targeted. The speed and certainty of the accusation is perfect. Sets up the nickname beat.

jokes.text: Marcel: 'You a regular Tonya Harding. I think we found your nickname.' / 'Other Tonya.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has accidentally injured Brenda
jokes.punchline: 'Other Tonya' — the callback to Marcel's earlier 'nickname will find you' speech pays off; the nickname that emerges from Ilana's accidental violence is perfectly absurd and also actually good
jokes.explanation: A standout moment. 'Other Tonya' is a brilliant nickname — it acknowledges the reference, differentiates her from the original (important: there is only one Tonya Harding, so you'd have to be 'Other'), and lands with perfect economy. The structural callback to Marcel's prophecy ('your nickname will find you') gives the whole scene a satisfying closed loop. Earns 8.5 on craft for the structure and the genius of 'Other' as a qualifier.

jokes.text: 'You almost had me fooled. Doing bodily harm... You a regular Tonya Harding.' / 'I think we found your nickname.' / 'Other Tonya.'
jokes.setup: Brenda has accused Ilana of assault; Marcel reassesses Ilana entirely
jokes.punchline: Marcel decides the Brenda incident was intentional Tonya Harding-style sabotage — so Ilana's nickname is 'Other Tonya' — paying off the earlier 'your nickname will find you' prophecy
jokes.explanation: Strong structural payoff — Marcel's earlier promise that 'your nickname will find you' is paid off here with maximum irony (Ilana earned her nickname through accidental kindness that looked like malice). 'Other Tonya' is a great nickname and the Tonya Harding reference is well-deployed. The callback makes this punch above its weight.

jokes.text: 'Strap on your strap-on, lace up your skates, 'cause it's time to dance.'
jokes.setup: Marcel promotes Ilana after the Tonya Harding incident
jokes.punchline: Marcel's motivational speech mixing skating imagery, strap-on sexual reference, and dance — delivered with complete conviction as if this is a normal pep talk
jokes.explanation: Only Marcel delivers this line. The juxtaposition of 'strap on your strap-on' (sexual) with 'lace up your skates' (Tonya callback) into 'it's time to dance' (completely unrelated activity) is the kind of surreal motivational speech that only exists in this show. Earns 8+ for character specificity and the audacity of the construction.

jokes.text: 'I'm upgrading you... to section two.'
jokes.setup: After Ilana accidentally injures Brenda and resigns herself to the job, Marcel rewards her.
jokes.punchline: A section upgrade delivered with the gravity of a military promotion — section two being apparently a meaningful distinction that Marcel bestows with great ceremony.
jokes.explanation: Marcel's pomposity applied to restaurant section assignments is consistently funny — the gravity he brings to logistics. The pause before 'to section two' mimics dramatic reveal structure.

jokes.text: Abbi demonstrating bungee fly exercise, gets launched: 'Uhh!' / Trey immediately: 'Abbi, you know what? Let me do that. Let's switch up. I just think seeing it from this angle might be, a little bit, ahem, safer.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is demonstrating an exercise for Shania. Something goes wrong with the bungee rig.
jokes.punchline: Trey's immediate intervention — 'from this angle might be safer' — is clearly an excuse to both preserve Abbi's dignity and get a better view, while the 'ahem' suggests the 'safer' angle has a different motivation.
jokes.explanation: More visual than textual — the 'Uhh!' bungee malfunction is physical comedy that lives on screen. Trey's diplomatic pivot ('safer') with the buried 'ahem' adds a layer of subtext. Competent physical comedy beat.

jokes.text: Shania dispenses relationship advice to Abbi about Trey and then improvises a love song from her own advice
jokes.setup: Shania is advising Abbi about chemistry with Trey
jokes.punchline: 'Damn, that's a good lyric' — Shania immediately recognizes her own advice ('when there's a spark, you gotta ride it') as song material and starts composing; she is constitutionally unable to not write a song
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best celebrity cameo jokes. The insight that Shania Twain would be incapable of giving relationship advice without immediately identifying it as lyrical material is a perfect celebrity-specific character observation. 'Damn, that's a good lyric' is the payoff — she's not just inspired, she's self-congratulatory. Earns 8.5 for character specificity and the meta-celebrity-songwriter angle.

jokes.text: 'Not all Canadians are prudes, you know.'
jokes.setup: Abbi seems surprised by Shania's directness about sex.
jokes.punchline: Shania pre-emptively defending Canadian sexuality as a category — as if this is a stereotype she regularly contends with.
jokes.explanation: Self-referential national identity joke from Shania Twain herself. The comedy is in the assumption she's defending — who was suggesting Canadian prudishness? — and the fact that she has this response ready implies she's encountered the stereotype before. Efficiently delivered.

jokes.text: Shania immediately starts composing and singing her new song in the gym, ending with: 'Fuck, I'm good.'
jokes.setup: 'When there's a spark, you gotta ride it' was identified as a potential lyric. Shania now performs it.
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck, I'm good.' — Shania's unselfconscious self-evaluation after her impromptu performance, with no audience beyond Abbi and a gym.
jokes.explanation: The setup and payoff are back-to-back which makes the escalation even faster. Shania composing a song in real time and then evaluating herself with 'fuck, I'm good' is perfect celebrity-ego comedy. The line is delivered (presumably) with total sincerity — she's not joking, she genuinely thinks she's good, and she's right. The confidence without any social modulation is what makes it funny and quotable. This is among the episode's best individual lines.

jokes.text: Trey, asked to say something meaningful to Abbi, says: 'Bazinga.'
jokes.setup: A tender moment; Abbi asks Trey to 'say it' — implying she wants to hear something real about their connection
jokes.punchline: 'Bazinga.' — Trey delivers Sheldon Cooper's catchphrase from The Big Bang Theory into what was set up to be a romantic confession moment; the gap is enormous and characterful
jokes.explanation: The episode's single biggest laugh moment. The structure is nearly perfect: the scene has been building toward an emotional declaration, Abbi's 'say it' raises the stakes to maximum, and then 'Bazinga' destroys all of it. The choice of 'Bazinga' specifically — a 2013-era Big Bang Theory catchphrase, the most culturally mid possible thing — is inspired. It reveals Trey as someone who cannot access genuine emotional expression and instead reaches for a meme. But it also might be his actual authentic response to romantic pressure. Maximum economy (one word). Earns its high score.

jokes.text: 'You're a good witch, not a bad witch.'
jokes.setup: Trey says something after the 'Bazinga' moment
jokes.punchline: Calling back to the opening 'witchy' conversation — Trey has apparently internalized this early-episode discussion, or the writers are giving the episode thematic closure with a callback; either way it's a sweet/funny beat
jokes.explanation: A warm callback that gives the episode structural roundness. Using Wizard of Oz/witch language to characterize Abbi's honesty arc is a clever piece of writing. Less of a laugh moment than a smile moment, but earns its place.

jokes.text: Ilana's character transformation revealed: 'You're a good witch, not a bad witch.'
jokes.setup: The cold open established 'witch' as a category of compliment.
jokes.punchline: Ilana applying the witch framework to evaluate her own moral character — paraphrasing Glinda from Wizard of Oz but in the context of restaurant bitchiness vs. genuine warmth.
jokes.explanation: The witch callback that reframes the whole Ilana-at-restaurant subplot through the episode's opening metaphor. 'Good witch, not a bad witch' lands because it ties together Glinda, Broad City's witch discourse, and Ilana's self-understanding. Well-placed structural callback.

jokes.text: Ilana (now as warm waitress): 'Can I interest anybody in a cognake? This is a cognac and sake cocktail.' / 'Is it good?' / 'Your eyes are stunning.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has abandoned 'bitch waitress' mode and returned to her natural warmth. She's describing a cocktail.
jokes.punchline: When asked 'is it good?' — a reasonable question about the drink — Ilana responds with 'your eyes are stunning,' completely non-responsive to the question but warmly complimentary in her own way.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Ilana joke. The question 'is it good?' gets the response 'your eyes are stunning' — Ilana's warmth is so reflexive that she can't answer a product question without first complimenting the human asking it. It's also practically useless as a sales pitch. This is exactly who Ilana is and could only be her. Excellent economy, original framing of a character trait as a service industry failure.

jokes.text: Ilana to another customer: 'With that body, please. / You could use a little more cushion for the pushin'. You're right. We'll have three wasabi soufflés.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's Kween-of-Mean mode is now repurposed as exuberant body-positivity selling.
jokes.punchline: 'Cushion for the pushin'' delivered as a sales pitch — followed by the customer agreeing and ordering food — collapses the distinction between flirting, affirmation, and waitressing.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's natural mode being accidentally excellent at sales — customers respond to her authentic affirmation better than they would to Marcel's practiced meanness. 'Cushion for the pushin'' as a body-positive food upsell is character-specific and funny.

jokes.text: Ilana's customer asks 'Are you guys high on weed?' and Ilana responds by ordering '17 spicy escargot wontons, stat'
jokes.setup: Ilana has apparently adopted a persona somewhere between mean and extremely nice
jokes.punchline: The customer correctly diagnoses that something is off; Ilana responds with a food order that is maximally specific and random
jokes.explanation: '17 spicy escargot wontons, stat' works as an absurdist non-sequitur. The specificity of 17 and the 'stat' (hospital-speak) makes it funnier than a generic order would be. Solid texture joke.

jokes.text: Ilana (warmly): 'I'm getting you 17 spicy escargot wontons, stat.'
jokes.setup: Responding to the 'are you high?' customer with her characteristic over-generous hospitality.
jokes.punchline: '17 spicy escargot wontons, stat' — the specificity of 17, the fusion absurdity of escargot wontons, and the urgency of 'stat' all at once.
jokes.explanation: The number 17 is the funniest number for a batch of escargot wontons — too many to be reasonable, too specific to be random. 'Stat' adds medical urgency to a food order. 'Escargot wontons' as a Sushi Mambeaux menu item is a nice background world-building joke.

jokes.text: Customer's response to Ilana: 'Thank you so much, Tim Foil.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been so warm and chaotic that the customer has apparently assigned her a nickname.
jokes.punchline: 'Tim Foil' — tin foil, but as a name. The customer has given Ilana a nickname in Marcel's style, and it's a terrible pun.
jokes.explanation: A callback to the nickname system, but from an unexpected direction — the customers are now doing it. 'Tim Foil' is a weak pun (tin foil) but earns its place as an accidental nickname finding Ilana through her warmth rather than her meanness. The reversal of the system's direction is the joke more than the pun itself.

jokes.text: 'I don't like what the new girl's done with this space. We gotta go somewhere else.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's warmth has transformed the restaurant's atmosphere from exclusive cruelty to chaotic affection.
jokes.punchline: A customer (presumably one who prefers the mean environment) complaining that Ilana's warmth has ruined the vibe — the joke being that someone preferred the hostile restaurant.
jokes.explanation: A nice satirical point — the clientele of Sushi Mambeaux chose it for the abuse, and Ilana's warmth has ruined their experience. Confirms that Marcel's customer base is self-selecting masochists. Solid irony.

jokes.text: Marcel announces he's taking the leftover food to 'the homeless of New York City' — then immediately J/K's to reveal he's going to Andy Cohen's party
jokes.setup: Marcel makes a touching gesture about feeding the homeless
jokes.punchline: 'J-K, Miss Thing. I'm going to a party at Andy Cohen's place.' — the complete reversal, the specific name-drop of Andy Cohen (fabulous Bravo host), and 'Miss Thing' as the mode of delivery
jokes.explanation: A great character joke. The structure (apparent generosity → 'J-K') is classic, but what makes it exceptional is Andy Cohen as the specific destination — it's not just a party, it's a party thrown by the most Marcel-adjacent person in real celebrity culture. The 'J-K, Miss Thing' is also peak Marcel. Earns 8+ for character specificity and the perfect choice of Andy Cohen.

jokes.text: Ilana says 'Good night, Dad' to Marcel as he leaves
jokes.setup: Marcel exits; Ilana says goodbye
jokes.punchline: 'Good night, Dad' — the Freudian slip/intentional statement that Marcel is a father figure to Ilana; Marcel's 'What'd you say?' shows he heard it
jokes.explanation: A sharp character insight delivered as a throwaway. Ilana finding a father figure in the imperious Marcel says a lot about what she needs from authority figures and relationships. The cut-off explanation ('the reason I took this job was because I wanted to reconnect with my fa...') is a perfectly handled reveal-and-evasion.

jokes.text: Trey gets hit in the genitals by gym equipment and reports 'I think I broke my dick'
jokes.setup: The bungee fly exercise, which Trey has taken over from Abbi for safety reasons, goes wrong
jokes.punchline: 'I think I broke my dick' — matter-of-fact self-diagnosis delivered in Trey's characteristic undramatic register
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy is predictable (guy gets hit in the groin) but the economy and Trey's delivery elevate it. 'I think I broke my dick' in that specific calm Trey voice is funnier than the same line screamed. Gets points for execution over concept.

jokes.text: Shania: 'Yeah, his dick is definitely broken. I'll figure it out.' / 'It's your standard penile fracture. We may have to operate.'
jokes.setup: Trey is in pain after the gym equipment injury
jokes.punchline: Shania assumes immediate medical authority — diagnosing a 'standard penile fracture' with complete calm; she's a pop star playing doctor with full confidence
jokes.explanation: 'Standard penile fracture' delivered by Shania Twain in her capacity as spontaneous celebrity medic is a strong absurdist beat. The 'I'll figure it out' preceding the diagnosis implies she's making this up as she goes, which is the funny part. Good Shania characterization.

jokes.text: 'It's your standard penile fracture. We may have to operate. Sorry, man.' / 'Ma'am?' / 'No, no, I said man.' / 'Oh. Okay, cool, thanks, Doctor.' / 'Oh, I'm not a doctor.'
jokes.setup: A paramedic arrives and assesses Trey's injury with professional authority.
jokes.punchline: Two-part: (1) Trey mishearing 'man' as 'ma'am' followed by the paramedic's immediate clarification, and (2) Trey calling them 'doctor' — revealing that their entire authoritative assessment was delivered without medical credentials.
jokes.explanation: A tight two-punch sequence. The ma'am/man mix-up is a quick gender beat, but the real joke is the paramedic's confident diagnosis ('it's your standard penile fracture, we may have to operate') being revealed as non-medical when they clarify 'I'm not a doctor.' The confident authority without credentials is the subversive point — delivered deadpan.

jokes.text: Trey: 'You're not the relationship type, right?' / Abbi: 'Yeah, totally.' — then immediately after, Abbi hits on paramedic Mike
jokes.setup: Trey characterizes Abbi as a non-relationship person; she agrees
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately asks paramedic Mike for his number at the next scene break — establishing she IS a relationship person, Trey has just never been right for her
jokes.explanation: A great structural comedy beat. The joke is in the immediacy — Trey's farewell speech is barely over before Abbi proves it wrong. The gap between her 'yeah, totally' and the next scene (asking for Mike's number) is the punchline. Strong character work: this definitively closes the Trey chapter while opening a new one, all within a single comedic beat. Earns 8+ for structure and character payoff.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'cause I am a relationship gal. Fully.' — delivered to Trey as he is being wheeled away
jokes.setup: Abbi has gotten Mike's number while Trey watches
jokes.punchline: The directness of 'I am a relationship gal. Fully.' — clarifying her identity to Trey as he's carted off on a stretcher is both comedically timed and character-defining
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback that gives Abbi character clarity. The 'Fully' as an addendum is the right word — it's commitment to the claim. The stretcher context makes it funnier (she's clarifying her romantic identity while her ex is being wheeled away injured).

jokes.text: Abbi shows Ilana photo evidence of Shania: 'This is Shania and I doing partner-heel taps. This one is Shania's feet under the bathroom stall. She wasn't even peeing. She was just watching an episode of Scandal.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is proving to Ilana that Shania was real
jokes.punchline: The bathroom stall photo — Abbi photographed Shania's feet under a stall, and the explanation that Shania was watching Scandal (not using the bathroom) makes it weirder and funnier
jokes.explanation: A great joke with excellent structural payoff. The image of Abbi photographing celebrity feet under a bathroom stall is already funny (she is that desperate for evidence). But 'she wasn't even peeing, she was just watching Scandal' layers celebrity-humanizing weirdness on top — Shania Twain uses the restroom as a TV room. This resolves the earlier 'BRB' bathroom exit. Strong craft, high impact, quotable. Earns 8+ for the specificity of Scandal and the absurdist celebrity detail.

jokes.text: 'If I need it, we have it. And if we ever have any other female friends, we will give it to them. It's available to them.'
jokes.setup: Discussion of the Plan B stockpile
jokes.punchline: The 'if we ever have any other female friends' caveat — they have a four-year supply but no one else to give it to; the accidental implication that they have no female friends beyond each other is both the laugh and the emotional truth of the show
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes of the episode and a perfect closing beat. 'If we ever have any other female friends' accidentally reveals the complete insularity of the Abbi-Ilana relationship — their world is just the two of them. It's funny, it's slightly sad, and it perfectly encapsulates what the show is about. The utilitarian 'it's available to them' is the right landing. High character integration, high originality in the framing, and deeply earned. Earns its 8.7.
